IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON NETWORK SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING, VOL. 13, 2026 6325
Movable Antenna-Enabled Secure Transmission for
Active RIS-Aided ISAC Systems
Hongyi Bian
, Feilong Zhao, Minghao Chen , Member, IEEE, Lingling Liu , Member, IEEE,
Yu Ya o
, Member, IEEE, Hao Jiang , Senior Member, IEEE, and Feng Shu , Senior Member, IEEE
Abstract—The integrated sensing and communication (ISAC)
technology empowered by movable antennas (MAs) has increased
the possibility of wireless communication development. In this
paper, we investigate a discrete MA-enabled active reconﬁgurable
intelligent surface (RIS)-aided ISAC system. In this system, the
position of discrete MAs can be dynamically adjusted to enhance
channel conditions, thereby improving the communication secu-
rity of legitimate users against potential eavesdropping risks from
target. In particular, we formulate an optimization problem for
the max-min secrecy rate (SR) by jointly optimizing beamforming
matrix, phase shift matrix and MA discrete positions. An efﬁcient
alternating optimization (AO) based on penalty-successive convex
approximation (SCA) and penalty-dual-decomposition (PDD) ap-
proach is proposed to obtain a high-quality initial solution for
beamforming matrix and phase shift in the non-convex problem.
Next, we propose a dueling double deep Q-Network (D3QN)-twin
delayed and deep deterministic policy gradient (TD3) method to
cope with the hybrid action space issue of the non-convex problem
with highly coupled variables. Numerical results demonstrate that
the introduction of MAs can effectively improve SR performance
compared to ﬁxed-position antenna (FPA) structure in RIS-aided
ISAC systems. In addition, the proposed AO-based D3QN-TD3
method validate the suitability and superiority compared to the
D3QN-TD3 method without AO algorithm.
Received 26 September 2025; revised 14 December 2025; accepted 21
January 2026. Date of publication 28 January 2026; date of current version
9 February 2026. This work was supported in part by the Hainan Province
Science and Technology Special Fund under Grant ZDYF2024GXJS292 and
Grant ZDYF2025GXJS014, in part by the National Natural Science Foundation
of China under Grant U22A2002, Grant 62561023, Grant 62561021, Grant
62471238, and Grant 62561024, in part by the National Key Research and
Development Program of China under Grant 2023YFF0612900, in part by the
Hainan Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant 625RC712,
and in part by the Scientiﬁc Research Fund Project of Hainan University
under Grant XJ2400010496. Recommended for acceptance by Dr. Weijie Yuan.
(Corresponding authors: Yu Yao; Feng shu.)

Digital Object Identiﬁer 10.1109/TNSE.2026.3657852
Index Terms—Movable antenna, integrated sensing and commu-
nication, reconﬁgurable intelligent surface, deep reinforcement
learning.
I. INTRODUCTION
A. Background
W
ITH the development of wireless communication in
sixth generation (6G) networks, the integrated sensing
and communication (ISAC) technology has received increasing
attention from both academia and industry, and are considered
one of the promising technologies for implementing the next
generation of wireless communication systems
[1], [2], [3].
Unlike separate communication systems and sensing systems,
ISAC systems combine sensing and communication functions in
one system through the same spectrum occupation, shared hard-
ware circuits, and uniﬁed waveform design
[4]. They not only
share the same resources, hardware facilities, and signal pro-
cessing modules, but also have the advantages of low hardware
cost, low power consumption, and high spectral efﬁciency
[5],
[6]. Most current research is conducted with ﬁxed numbers and
positions of base station antennas, which results in wireless net-
works being unable to ﬂexibly allocate their antenna resources
based on the uneven distribution of user space. At the same
time, current researches improve performance by using more
ﬁxed position antenna (FPA) at the cost of increasing hardware
costs and energy consumption. Therefore, FPA structure may
not meet the high-performance expectations of future 6G ISAC
networks
[7].
Compared to traditional FPA structures, movable antenna
(MA) technology can ﬂexibly adapt to multipath channel char-
acteristics in dynamic environments by changing the shape,
position, polarization, etc. of the transmitting/receiving antenna,
resulting in a higher signal-to-noise ratio at the receiving end
and improving the reliability of the communication system
[8],
[9], [10]. Speciﬁcally, the concept of MA introduces a new
dimension of spatial degrees of freedom (DoF) for wireless
communication systems, making beamforming more precise and
further improving system spectral and energy efﬁciency
[11].
In addition to improve channel power gain, beamforming and
spatial multiplexing of MA systems can provide additional DoF.
Especially by jointly designing the position and beamforming of
MAs, more ﬂexible beamforming can be achieved
[12]. Specif-
ically, optimizing the position of antenna array elements can
enhance the correlation between guidance vectors in different
2327-4697 © 2026 IEEE. All rights reserved, including rights for text and data mining, and training of artiﬁcial intelligence and similar technologies.
Personal use is permitted, but republication/redistribution requires IEEE permission. See https://www.ieee.org/publications/rights/index.html for more information.
Authorized licensed use limited to: Xi'an Univ of Posts & Telecom. Downloaded on March 17,2026 at 01:10:53 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.
6326 IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON NETWORK SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING, VOL. 13, 2026
directions, thereby achieving larger beamforming gains in all
directions
[13].
For spatial multiplexing, optimizing the position of MA can
reshape the channel matrix of MIMO systems, thus further
improving channel capacity and spectral efﬁciency compared
to traditional FPA MIMO systems
[14].Ref.[14] conﬁrmed
that compared to traditional FPA systems, MA systems can
signiﬁcantly improve the channel capacity of the system by
reshaping the channel matrix through optimizing antenna posi-
tions. In addition, MAs can suppress multi-user interference by
ﬂexibly conﬁguring antenna positions. Reference
[15] proposes
a new multiple access method, namely ﬂuid antenna multiple
access. Speciﬁcally, compared to traditional multiple access
methods that require complex signal processing and encoding,
ﬂuid antenna multiple access only requires moving the antennas
of each user to the signal to interference ratio region to effectively
suppress multi-user interference without the need for complex
signal processing. Furthermore, as space-air-ground integrated
communication technology emerges as a key research direction
for future communication development, MA technology can
adapt to and optimize channel conditions by adjusting antenna
positions and angles
[16]. This capability enables it to better
address the complex and dynamic communication network en-
vironments of space-air-ground integration, achieving robust
system performance improvements under intricate and variable
transmission conditions
[17], [18]. Therefore, as one of the key
technologies in 6G mobile communications, MA technology can
organically integrate with various 6G mobile communication
application scenarios. Leveraging its inherent technical advan-
tages, it provides momentum for the development of 6G mobile
communication technology, thereby accelerating its rapid ad-
vancement and widespread application.
With the ISAC technology being identiﬁed as one of the
typical application scenarios for 6G mobile networks, MA
technology can signiﬁcantly enhance both communication and
sensing performance by leveraging spatial DoF without increas-
ing the number of antennas or radio frequency components,
thus demonstrating signiﬁcant application potential in future 6G
networks
[19], [20]. In addition, the change in its spatial position
effectively increases the antenna aperture, which can improve
sensing accuracy. Therefore, it has important application poten-
tial in future 6G networks. Research has shown that under given
signal-to-noise ratio conditions, the Cramér-Rao bound (CRB)
of the mean square error of directional angle estimation is mainly
determined by the variance of multiple antenna positions. There-
fore, without changing the number of antennas, increasing the
range of antenna movement can signiﬁcantly improve the angle
estimation performance
[21]. However, due to the different goals
of communication and sensing, the position and orientation of
the MA needs to be designed reasonably to achieve the best
compromise of synesthesia performance.
Moreover, in order to further explore the DoF in the spa-
tial domain to improve communication performance, MA is
introduced into Reconﬁgurable intelligent surfaces (RIS)
[22],
[23], [24]. By ﬂexibly adjusting the topological structure of RIS
reﬂection units, an intelligent and controllable wireless channel
propagation environment can be further achieved
[25]. RIS has
became a revolutionary method for improving additional DoF
due to the ability to intelligently create favorable propagation
environments and achieve satisfactory performance
[26], [27].
Speciﬁcally, Reference
[26] considered a scenario where the
transmit antenna, receive antenna, and RIS can rotate, derives
an upper bound on the system throughput capacity under the
Rayleigh channel, and solves for the optimal rotation angles
of the transmit/receive antennas and RIS based on this. The
results indicated that optimizing the rotation angles of the RIS
signiﬁcantly improves the performance of the communication
system. In addition to rotation, Reference
[27] analyzed RIS
composed of linear movable arrays and points out that by
optimizing the positions of the individual elements of the RIS
the phase shift effects caused by different transmission angles
and arrival angles at the RIS end can be effectively eliminated,
signiﬁcantly improving the system’s reachability.
In addition, due to the shared spectrum and broadcast char-
acteristics of wireless transmission, MA-enabled systems face
unique security challenges. Undoubtedly, secure communica-
tion issues are crucial for the design of MA-enabled sys-
tems
[28], [29], [30]
. To address this issue, Physical Layer
Security (PLS) has been used by researchers to handle secure
communication problems
[31], [32], [33]. PLS is an important
communication security technology aimed at enhancing the se-
curity of communication systems by utilizing the characteristics
of wireless channels and physical layer technology to prevent
unauthorized users from eavesdropping, interfering, or tamper-
ing with communication data
[34], [35], [36], [37]. Compared
to traditional cryptographic based security mechanisms, PLS
provides an additional security guarantee by directly utilizing
the physical characteristics of signal transmission to ensure
communication security
[35]. Speciﬁcally, by optimizing the
position of the transmitting antenna and changing the channel
state information, the eavesdropper’s channel is placed in a deep
fading state, thereby enhancing PLS
[38].
In summary, to fully leverage the advantages of MA in com-
munication security, we must focus on optimizing the best solu-
tion for MA’s selectable positions and overcome the limitations
of static beamforming design. By developing an efﬁcient and
ﬂexible MA position selection scheme, we can not only fully
unlock MA’s potential to enhance communication capacity but
also increase DoF and ensure communication security.
B. Contributions and Organization
Based on the aforementioned discussion, we study the discrete
MA-enabled secure transmission for RIS-aided ISAC systems.
Unlike the optimization of continuous MA positions, moving
discrete antenna positions can reduce the complexity of me-
chanical systems and facilitate practical deployment. In ad-
dition, most optimization schemes were limited to traditional
optimization methods and failed to derive solutions that adapt to
dynamic environments. To this end, we are committed to design-
ing an efﬁcient joint traditional optimization methods and deep
reinforcement learning (DRL) algorithm to address the secure
transmission issues in ISAC systems. The main contributions
are listed as follows:
Authorized licensed use limited to: Xi'an Univ of Posts & Telecom. Downloaded on March 17,2026 at 01:10:53 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.
BIAN et al.: MOVABLE ANTENNA-ENABLED SECURE TRANSMISSION FOR ACTIVE RIS-AIDED ISAC SYSTEMS 6327
r
In this paper, we propose a novel discrete MA-enabled
secure transmission for active RIS-aided ISAC systems,
where the system ﬁrstly considering the innovation of
simultaneously optimizing the discrete positions of trans-
mitting and receiving antennas at BS. The max-min secrecy
rate (SR) optimization problem is formulated by jointly
optimizing the transmit beamforming of BS, the phase shift
of RIS and the position of MAs, respectively. Moreover, the
communication requirements of users are ensured while
the constraints of the CRB for target estimation, power
consumption, and the positions of MAs are maintained
according to the problem formulation.
r
In order to optimize variable processing for complex con-
tinuous and discrete actions, we propose an alternating op-
timization (AO)-based dueling Ddouble deep Q-Network
(D3QN)-twin delayed and deep deterministic policy gradi-
ent (TD3) method to solve the established long-term plan-
ning problem. Speciﬁcally, to address the tightly coupled
challenges of multi-antenna array positioning, transmit
beamforming, and phase shifting, the AO-based D3QN-
TD3 secure transmission scheme ensures the security of
the ISAC system by learning effective strategies.
r
We construct the AO-based algorithm to optimize both
transmit beamforming and the reﬂection coefﬁcient of the
RIS. Speciﬁcally, the transmit beamforming is optimized
by adopting the penalty-successive convex approximation
(SCA) method to relax rank-one constraint. Moreover, we
develop a penalty-dual-decomposition (PDD) algorithm to
deal with the complex quartic expressions in RIS phase
shift optimization.
r
The comprehensive simulation results demonstrate bene-
ﬁts of the MA-enable ISAC systems, which indicate that
the MA-enabled ISAC system outperforms the FPA-based
ISAC system due to the spatial diversity gain and addi-
tional DoF introduced by antenna position optimization. In
addition, expanding the MA moving region area increases
the equivalent array aperture, thereby effectively extending
the coverage range of the ISAC system without adding
more antennas. Furthermore, compared to the traditional
D3QN-TD3 approach, the AO-based D3QN-TD3 method
achieves signiﬁcant improvements in safety performance
across all aspects.
C. Organization and Notation
The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. The
related work is comprehensively described in Section
II.In
Section III, we present the MA-enabled Active RIS-aided secure
ISAC system and the related problem formulation. Section
IV
provides the efﬁcient methods to solve the max-min SR opti-
mization problem. The simulation results demonstrate the ef-
fectiveness of the proposed method are presented in Section V.
Lastly, Section
VI provides the concluding remarks of this paper.

tian transpose, and vectorization operations are denoted by (·)

,
(·)

, (·)

, and vec(·), respectively. |·|, ·, and ·
F
stand
for the norm, l
2
-norm, and Frobenius-norm, respectively. A

I{·} indicate the real and imaginary components of a complex
value, respectively. ⊗ stands for the Kronecker product, I
N
corresponds to a N-dimensional identity matrix, and diag(a)
denotes a diagonal matrix. In addition, CN(0,σ
2
) refers to the
complex normal distribution with zero-mean and variance σ
2
,
and E{·}signiﬁes the expectation operator. [·]
+
is a value greater
than or equal to zero.
II. RELATED WORK
In this section, we discuss the current state of MA technology
research with a focus on MA techniques. First, we analyze
the signiﬁcant potential for MA technology to integrate with
ISAC and RIS technologies, highlighting its advantages over
FPA. Additionally, we examine the security implications of MA
technology applications and the need for speciﬁc optimization
methods to address these challenges.
The current ISAC research is usually conducted based on FPA,
and considerable exploration and discovery have been made
from RIS technology and safety considerations. Zhu et al.
[39]
have applied a deep deterministic policy gradient (DDPG) and
soft actor-critic (SAC) algorithm to maximize the long-term
average security rate in the simultaneous transmitting and re-
ﬂecting RIS (STAR-RIS) aided ISAC secure system. Kumar
et al.
[40] addressed a beampattern maximization problem in a
secure active RIS-enabled ISAC system via a high-performance
solution. Liu et al.
[41] adopted a DRL algorithm to maxi-
mize the achievable SR of all the legitimate users in a RIS-
assisted ISAC system. Chu et al.
[42] leveraged the RIS to
assist legitimate wireless communication while enhancing the
sensing performance in a secure RIS-aided ISAC system. Guo
et al.
[43] have achieved a high-quality sensing beampattern
while meeting covertness and security communication require-
ments in a STAR-RIS assisted ISAC system. Yao et al.
[44]
proposed a novel secure scheme for transmitting conﬁdential
information and performing radar sensing in the RIS-enabled
vehicle-to-everything networks. Li et al.
[45] ignored the issue
of internal eavesdropping and assume that legitimate users are
not malicious. Yang et al.
[46] focused on security beamforming
technology in RIS-assisted ISAC systems, with an emphasis on
sensing accuracy and eavesdropper localization. Yang et al.
[47]
proposed a novel continuous interference cancellation scheme
that ensures the integrity of legitimate user links while utilizing
radar signals to disrupt potential eavesdropping behavior. Zhang
et al.
[48] investigated an active RIS-assisted ISAC system
to maximize the SR by adopting the alternating optimization
algorithm. However, the aforementioned studies are all based on
traditional FPA structure, which relies on increasing the number
of antennas and radio frequencies to enhance service quality,
leading to signiﬁcant increases in hardware costs and power
consumption. By leveraging spatial DoF, MA technology can
substantially improve communication and sensing performance
without adding more antennas or RFs, thus holding signiﬁcant
application potential in future 6G networks.
Authorized licensed use limited to: Xi'an Univ of Posts & Telecom. Downloaded on March 17,2026 at 01:10:53 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.
6328 IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON NETWORK SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING, VOL. 13, 2026
The introduction of MA technology provides a new paradigm
for the development of future communication. For secure com-
munication, the existing MA technologies are mainly studied
in combination with PLS, like direction modulation and covert
communication. By optimizing the position of the antenna,
potential better channels can be selected for data transmission,
thereby enhancing the secure transmission rate. The paper pro-
posed a joint optimization scheme for transmit precoding and
MA positioning to maximize SR in MIMO systems, demonstrat-
ing signiﬁcant security improvements over FPAs. In
[49], a joint
optimization scheme for transmit precoding, artiﬁcial noise,
and MA positioning was proposed to maximize SR in MIMO
systems, demonstrating signiﬁcant security improvements over
FPAs. Reference
[50] demonstrated that MAs can signiﬁcantly
expand the achievable SR region for physical-layer service
integration compared to FPAs through joint beamforming and
antenna positioning optimization. For the position optimization
of MAs, in
[51], [52], and [53], the gradient descent algorithm,
particle swarm optimization algorithm, and block coordinate
descent algorithm were respectively proposed to enhance com-
munication security performance. In
[54], considering hardware
damage and imperfect channel estimation, a uniform packet
and non-uniform packet scheme was proposed to optimize the
position of the MA, achieving higher security performance than
the traditional FPAs under discrete position constraints.
However, the aforementioned works have studied the con-
tinuously adjustable positions of MAs in the ISAC system. For
example, Zhang et al.
[55] addressed the optimization of antenna
positions in a continuous manner, and demonstrated that under
given antenna counts and power budgets, even minor adjust-
ments to the position of a mobile antenna array can signiﬁcantly
enhance the performance of integrated communication and sens-
ing systems. Furthermore, Lyu et al.
[56] further investigated the
joint design of continuous antenna position and beamforming in
a MA-aided ISAC system, aiming to maximize the weighted
sum of communication rate and sensing mutual information
under a given total transmit power budget. Considering practical
scenarios for ISAC, enhancing secure communication and target
sensing through the optimization of MAs under discrete position
constraints is a key issue
[57]. Some studies have attempted to
address the issue of MA discretization in position selection [58].
Shao et al. [58] proposed six-dimensional (6D) position and
rotation of 6D MA surface to maximize network capacity. Zou
et al.
[59] achieved a 33% reduction in transmission power
compared to traditional FPAs by jointly optimizing the discrete
positions and beamforming of a one-dimensional MA, while
meeting communication and sensing performance requirements.
Wang et al.
[60] has further investigated a two-dimensional
MA-assisted pan-sensing integrated system. By employing deep
reinforcement learning to jointly optimize discrete antenna po-
sitions and precoding schemes, it signiﬁcantly enhances multi-
user communication rates while satisfying sensing requirement
constraints. Mei et al.
[61] transformed the continuous antenna
position optimization problem into a discrete sampling point
selection problem based on point by point channel information,
and developed a graph based optimization technique to obtain
the optimal solution.
For the ISAC system assisted by MAs, the trade-off between
communication and sensing has been studied. By optimizing
the positions of the MAs, existing works have shown that the
performance of communication and sensing can be enhanced.
In
[62], by minimizing the CRB for sensing under communi-
cation constraints, the MA-based ISAC system achieves sub-
stantial performance gains over conventional FPA setups. Pang
et al.
[63] revealed the potential uses of movable components
through performance analysis, and then designed a joint beam-
forming and component position optimization scheme, proving
that this scheme can improve communication rate and sensing
accuracy. Ding et al.
[64] proposed a dual layer random position
algorithm to solve the problem of maximizing the weighted sum
of sensing rate and communication rate in MA assisted systems.
Qin et al.
[65] formulated the CRB minimization problem in
the MA-aided multiuser ISAC system. Xiu et al.
[66] aimed
to maximize capacity by jointly optimizing MA positions and
beamforming in the MA-assisted low-altitude vehicle ISAC
model. Fang et al.
[67] has provided a new deﬁnition for the mode
of antenna selection in the simultaneous sensing and backscatter
communication in cluttered environments. In addition, interest-
ing technologies for the assistance of MAs considering safety
performance in ISAC systems have been studied. In
[68], a prox-
imal policy optimization algorithm was proposed to maximize
the SR while ensuring sensing performance in the MA-aided
ISAC systems under imperfect eavesdropper’s Channel State
Information. Employing a two-layer penalty-based algorithm,
the proposed MA-assisted RIS-ISAC system in
[69] is shown
to signiﬁcantly outperform conventional FPA-based systems in
enhancing secure performance by jointly optimizing beamform-
ing, RIS coefﬁcients, and MA positions.
In addition, the RIS assisted MA system can signiﬁcantly
enhance the transmission capacity of existing communication
systems, providing a new paradigm for wireless communication
in complex dynamic scenarios. Lyu et al.
[70] ﬁrstly designed a
robust transmission scheme by combining RIS and MA to maxi-
mize the primary rate while ensuring the secondary transmission
quality. Compared to traditional RIS assisted FPAs,
[71] revealed
that MA enabled RIS can optimize antenna position to improve
channel capacity. Wei et al.
[72] has provided the conclusion
that the performance gain of MA relative to traditional FPAs
may decrease in the presence of RIS passive beamforming. Hu
et al.
[27] designed the optimal non-uniform discrete phase shift
for RIS, which can offset the phase distribution between different
levels of channels. Of course, the combination of MA and RIS
also provides a fresh approach to in-depth research on ISAC
systems. A novel RIS structure combining MA under spherical
wave propagation conditions has been proposed in
[73], which
optimizes the weighted sum rate of communication users while
ensuring perceptual performance. Wu et al.
[74] studied a two-
dimensional (2D) MA array and RIS assisted ISAC system to
address the issue of communication and sensing performance in
dead zones.
Although all current MAs utilize micro-electromechanical
systems or stepper motors to achieve high precision, miniatur-
ization, and low power consumption, in practical applications,
the movement and repositioning of MAs require additional
Authorized licensed use limited to: Xi'an Univ of Posts & Telecom. Downloaded on March 17,2026 at 01:10:53 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.
BIAN et al.: MOVABLE ANTENNA-ENABLED SECURE TRANSMISSION FOR ACTIVE RIS-AIDED ISAC SYSTEMS 6329
power to operate mechanical components, which undoubtedly
leads to increased energy consumption. Furthermore, all existing
studies assume that the latency of MA movement is tolerable
and negligible. However, for broadband systems or even large
transmitter area systems, the latency of MA movement can
also impose limitations on system performance improvement.
Therefore, research on the latency and energy efﬁciency issues
of MA devices is an unavoidable challenge for the practical
application of MA technology in future mobile communication
scenarios. Wei et al.
[75] ﬁrstly proposed a detailed power
consumption model for stepper motor-driven multi-MA systems
based on fundamental motor theory, which accounts for the
nonlinear relationship between mechanical energy consumption
and movement speed, and apply it to the joint optimization
problem for energy efﬁciency maximization. By analyzing the
actual time delay and energy consumption caused by antenna
movement, Ding et al.
[76] investigated the energy efﬁciency
maximization problem in MA-assisted multi-user uplink com-
munication systems, derives the upper bound of energy efﬁ-
ciency, and provides important references for practical system
deployment. Li et al.
[77] explicitly focused on MA movement
delay and ﬁnds that the movement delay decreases as the max-
imum speed of MA increases. However, under the same speed
constraints, by jointly optimizing matching and trajectory, the
time wasted due to avoidance and unreasonable scheduling can
be signiﬁcantly reduced. Wei et al.
[78] speciﬁcally established
a mechanical power consumption model for the single MA
system, and demonstrated that MA can still outperform tradi-
tional FPA systems even when accounting for additional power
consumption, by jointly optimizing the MA’s position, moving
speed, and transmit power to maximize energy efﬁciency. Chen
et al.
[79] demonstrated that ﬂexibly adjusting antenna positions
to exploit spatial DoF can effectively enhance desired signals and
suppress interference. Furthermore, when the antenna move-
ment area expands beyond a certain size, the improvement in
system energy efﬁciency tends to saturate.
It is also concerning that robust optimization of MA positions
based on traditional optimization methods struggles to handle
fast-paced dynamic scenarios due to multiple uncertainties. Fur-
thermore, it is difﬁcult to efﬁciently process high-dimensional
actions and complex state spaces. Artiﬁcial intelligence-assisted
technologies such as DRL are also being vigorously applied
to MA-assisted communication systems. An AO-based deep
Q-network (DQN) algorithm method was proposed in
[80] to
design the moving trajectories of MAs, achieving a covert rate
improvement. Weng et al.
[81] proposed a multi agent deep
deterministic policy gradient (MADDPG) algorithm to optimize
real-time transmission beamforming and antenna movement
strategies, maximizing the sum rate under imperfect channel
state information. Li et al.
[82] proposed a multi-agent twin
delayed deep deterministic policy gradient method to maximize
the sum of achievable rates for all users in the MA-enabled
cell-free communication network system. Xie et al.
[83] pro-
posed a heterogeneous multi-agent deep deterministic policy
gradient algorithm to manage beamforming and antenna posi-
tioning tasks in MA-aided systems. Shao et al.
[84] proposed
a low-complexity Actor-Critic (A2C) based DRL algorithm to
Fig. 1. Illustration of the MA-enabled active RIS-aided ISAC model.
address the issue of coupled position and rotation constraints
attributed to 6DMA.
Table
I summarizes the classic literature comparisons to our
work.
III. S
YSTEM MODEL
In this paper, we consider a MA-enabled active RIS-aided
ISAC system as illustrated in Fig.
1, where a dual-function BS
employs two MA uniform planner array (UPA) for transmitting
and receiving signals. Speciﬁcally, BS transmits information to
K single-antenna users, while a single-antenna eavesdropping
target to be sensed is hovering nearby to intercept their data.
The M discrete transmit and N discrete receive MAs can be
adjusted simultaneously within a given 2D transmit and receive
area, respectively. Assuming the direct link from BS to the
eavesdropping target is unavailable due to blockages, the active
RIS is responsible for establishing a robust virtual line-of-sight
(LoS) link to ensure communication and sensing performance.
To maximize the potential of the MA-enabled system under
consideration, the perfect channel state information regarding
the transmitter region is assumed achievable at BS.

2
t
) denotes the complex-valued channel

the target sensing, communication signals can also be used as
sensing signals. The BS works in full duplex mode and receives
the echo signal of the target through the BS-RIS-target-RIS-BS
in four hops. It should be noted that the noise signal z
0
(t) is
ignored due to its extremely weak power when it reaches the base
station through multi-stage attenuation. Therefore, the received
echo signal by the BS at the t-th time slot is denoted as

D. Problem Formulation
In this paper, we aim to maximize the minimum SR by jointly
designing transmit beamforming W, active RIS phase shift
matrix Θ and the transmit and receive MA position selection
matrix A and B while assuring the QoS requirements of commu-
nication users. The SR optimization problem can be formulated
as

(34)
It can be noted that the constraint
(33b) ensures the QoS
requirements of communication users.
(33d) and (33e) represent
the power constraints, where P
BS
and P
RIS
are the maximum
power threshold at the BS and active RIS, respectively.
(33f)
is the maximum amplitude constraint of the active RIS. (33g),
(33h), and (33i) ensures compliance with the selection require-
ments for the MA position and the distance constraints between
elements.
(33j), (33k), and (33l) also have the same selection
requirements for receiving MA antennas.
Due to antenna mobility and channel variability, the en-
vironment is highly dynamic, posing challenges for conven-
tional optimization methods. Moreover, the original optimiza-
tion problem cannot directly handle constraints
(33i) and (33l)
using traditional optimization methods, and directly using DRL
algorithms will result in extremely slow convergence and the
ultimate outcome cannot be guaranteed. Therefore, we extracts
Authorized licensed use limited to: Xi'an Univ of Posts & Telecom. Downloaded on March 17,2026 at 01:10:53 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.
BIAN et al.: MOVABLE ANTENNA-ENABLED SECURE TRANSMISSION FOR ACTIVE RIS-AIDED ISAC SYSTEMS 6333
Fig. 2. Flowchart of the AO-based D3QN-TD3 method.
an optimization subproblem P
1
concerning the optimization
variable W and Θ from the overall optimization problem
P
1
:max
W,Θ
min
k∈K
R
sec,k
(35a)
s.t.
(33b)−(33f). (35b)
In addition, we employs a novel hybrid method combining
AO-based approach and DRL technology to tackle complex
challenges in Problem
(33).
IV. P
ROPOSED AO-BASED D3QN-TD3 METHOD
A. AO-Based DRL Structure
Due to the particularity of MA position optimization, tra-
ditional optimization methods are difﬁcult to solve the MA
optimization of discrete positions. Therefore, in order to obtain
better MA selection, we adopt DRL for antenna optimization.
Considering that DRL may be difﬁcult to converge to the optimal
solution in continuous space optimization, we adopt an AO algo-
rithm to optimize beamforming and phase parameters, thereby
effectively improving the convergence efﬁciency towards the
optimal solution. The ﬂowchart of the AO-based D3QN-TD3
method is shown in Fig.
2.
1) MDP Model: Given the complex and dynamic nature of
the proposed MA-enabled active RIS-aided ISAC system, a
DRL method was adopted to address this challenge. By for-
malizing the optimization problem of transmission rate as a
Markov decision process, the complexity of the optimization
problem was effectively simpliﬁed. Within this framework, the
semantic communication network is enhanced through the col-
laborative interaction between BS and RIS, creating a dynamic
environment. The BS control center acts as an intelligent agent
responsible for decision-making and regulating the system’s
behavior. The problem involves various elements such as the
environment, intelligent agents, state space, action space, state
transition probabilities, and reward functions. These elements
are crucial for accurately modeling and solving the Markov
decision process, as detailed below.
r
State space S: The state of the system at time slot n is
deﬁned as
s
n
= {{R
sec,k
}
n
, {CRB
θ
}
n
, {G
t
}
n
, {G
r
}
n
}, (36)
where {R
sec,k
}
n
is the security rate of Bob k, {CRB
θ
}
n
is the estimated CRB, {G
t
}
n
and {G
r
}
n
are the channel
matrices of the BS-RIS transmitting and RIS-BS receiving
paths, respectively. s
n
∈S, S is the set of all state space.
r
Action space A: Due to the fact that optimization problems
involve a mixture of discrete and continuous actions, the
action space is divided into two parts. One part is used
for discrete actions, including the transmit and receive MA
position selection matrices A
n
and B
n
, and the other part
is used for continuous actions, including the beamforming
vector W
n
of the base station and the phase shift Θ
n
of
the active RIS. The state of the system at time slot n is
deﬁned as
a
n
= {W
n
, Θ
n
, A
n
, B
n
}, (37)
where W
n
is the transmit beamforming matrix, Θ
n
is
the active RIS phase shift matrix and A
n
and B
n
are
the transmit and receive MA position selection matrices,
respectively. a
n
∈A, A is the set of all state space.
r
Reward function R: The reward of the system at time slot
n is deﬁned by r
n
, which is based on MAs’ selection of
antenna location and quantiﬁed information feedback from
the environment, and can be calculated as
r
n
= R + λ
1

c,k

, (38)

and λ

are constant coefﬁcients, setting the sum
SR, each user achievable rate, and radar estimation error

(40)
The goal of MDP is to maximize the long-term accumulative
reward, which can be expressed as
R
n
=
n

i=0
γ
i
r
i
. (41)
When the optimal robust SR performance is achieved, the
system will continue to maintain the state to maximize the
cumulative expected reward. Therefore, by utilizing the feature
of maximizing the cumulative expectation of rewards in MDP,
MA optimization will converge to the optimal selection position
and remain optimal at this position.
B. Proposed AO-Based Algorithm
1) Problem Reformulation: To begin with, we notice that
the max-min objective function
(35a) is non-smooth, hence,
an auxiliary variable ξ is introduced. Moreover, an auxiliary
variable J ∈ C
2×2
is introduced to handle the CRB constraint,
and the constraint
(33c) can be converted into the following two
constraints:
Tr(J

) ≤ δ, (42a)

where ξ is an auxiliary variable to ensure the equivalence
between constraint
(43b) and the objective function of the
original optimization problem. Notably, Problem
(43) aimed at
maximizing the long-term sum SR, which is non-convex and
characterized by numerous time-varying optimization variables.
2) Optimization of J: Given other variables, the optimization
problem for updating J can be formulated as
max
J,ξ

We observe that Problem (44) is a semideﬁnite program-
ming (SDP) problem, which can be readily solved by standard
algorithms.

the SINR constraint of k-th user is reformed as

Similar to the Problem
(48), there exists the rank-one con-
straint (76c) that difﬁcult to directly optimize in a convex
method. When we ignore the constraint
(76c), the original
optimization Problem
(79) is a convex second order cone pro-
gramming Problem and using CVX solvers to solve.
C. Proposed D3QN-TD3 Method
For the MA position optimization, it is a mixed integer non-
linear programming problem, so the non-convex Problem
(33) is
difﬁcult to solve using conventional methods. Therefore, DRL is
introduced to solve the problem. DRL, as a powerful nonlinear
optimization tool, can explore complex and high-dimensional
state spaces and gradually optimize the solution to the prob-
lem through learning strategies. This section proposes a hybrid
DRL framework to address the joint optimization problem of
BS beamforming matrix, RIS phase shift, and MA position
selection. Due to the mixed discrete-continuous action space,
conventional DRL methods struggle to solve this problem. The
D3QN-TD3 algorithm decomposes the entire ISAC secure trans-
mission problem into an external Markov Decision Process with
discrete action space solved by D3QN, and an internal Markov
Decision Process with continuous action space solved by TD3.
Speciﬁcally, D3QN handles the discrete actions for antenna
position selection, while TD3 manages the continuous actions
for beamforming and RIS phase shifts. The hybrid D3QN-TD3
model undergoes centralized training through a uniﬁed reward
signal to coordinate both discrete and continuous policies. Af-
ter training completion, the agent can execute policies in a
decentralized manner to minimize communication overhead.
Therefore, a D3QN-TD3 algorithm is introduced to enhance
training stability and accelerate training speed.
1) Reinforcement Learning Process: To learn from the un-
known environment, the agent needs to balance exploration and
exploitation. That is to say, the agent will explore unknown
knowledge by taking action randomly and exploit the learned
knowledge by greedily taking action with the maximum Q-
value. Herein, the objective of agent is to maximize the long-term
cumulative reward for the MA position selection policy π.
At time slot n, given state s
n
and action a
n
, the Q-function
with policy π is deﬁned as
Q(s
n
, a
n
)=E
π
[R
n
|s
n
, a
n
] . (80)
The optimal policy π
∗
can be obtained by maximizing
Q(s
n
, a
n
) as follows:
π
∗
← argmax
a
n
∼π
Q(s
n
, a
n
). (81)
According to the Bellman criterion, the optimal Q-value is
estimated as
Q
∗
(s
n
, a
n
)=r
n
+ γ max Q
∗
(s
n+1
, a
n+1
) , (82)
which can be updated by
Q(s
n
, a
n
) ← (1 − α)Q(s
n
, a
n
)+αQ
∗
(s
n
, a
n
), (83)
where α is the learning rate.
Theoretically, we can derive π
∗
by iteratively updating
(83).
However, due to the dynamic and random nature of the envi-
ronment, iteratively determining π
∗
within an exploding state
space is impractical. Therefore, integrating deep learning with
reinforcement learning to address exploding state spaces repre-
sents the most effective solution currently available.
2) D3QN and TD3 Algorithm a) D3QN: The D3QN algo-
rithm integrates DDQN and dueling DQN to improve the train-
ing performance of MDP problems. Speciﬁcally, the dueling
DQN architecture employs two sets of fully connected layer
networks in place of the traditional single neural network, en-
abling parallel estimation of both the state value function and the
advantage function. Furthermore, the dual network mechanism
incorporates an evaluation network and a target network, which
mitigates the coupling between the action selection and the
evaluation of action values, thereby addressing the prevalent
issue of overestimation found in conventional DQN algorithms.
Due to channel time-varying characteristics and high-
dynamic antenna adjustment strategies, the state space exhibits
exponential growth, causing the Q-values stored in the Q-table
to surge dramatically. This signiﬁcantly reduces the learning
efﬁciency of MA discrete optimal position selection, transmit
beamforming, and RIS deployment strategies. To address this,
we employ a deep neural network (DNN) with interconnected
weighted neurons to approximate the Q-values, i.e.,
Q(s
n
, a
n
) ≈ Q(s
n
, a
n
)|θ), (84)
where θ denotes the model parameter of DNNs.
As Q
∗
(s
n
, a
n
) increases with Q(s
n
, a
n
) increasing, there
will be oscillations and divergence. To address this problem, we
employ the double architecture with estimation Q-network and
target Q-network to approximate Q(s
n
, a
n
) and Q
∗
(s
n
, a
n
),
respectively. The estimation Q-network and target Q-network
employ the same structure but with different parameters to gen-
erate Q-values. Speciﬁcally, at time t, the estimation Q-network
utilizes the state s
n
to generate the Q-value Q(s
n
, a
n
) and the
action a
n
, where θ represents the parameter of the estimation

6338 IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON NETWORK SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING, VOL. 13, 2026
Q-network. Similarly, at time n +1, the target Q-network uti-
lizes the state s
n+1
to generate the Q-value Q
∗
(s
n+1
, a
n+1
|θ
′
)
and the action a
n+1
, where θ
′
represents the parameter of the
target Q-network. According to
(82),wehave
Q
∗
(s
n
, a
n
|θ
′
)=r
p
(t)+γ max Q
∗
(s
n+1
, a
n+1
|θ
′
). (85)
In this way, the temporal difference error at time n is
calculated as
δ = Q
∗
(s
n
, a
n
|θ
′
) − Q(s
n
, a
n
|θ). (86)
With (86), we employ stochastic gradient descent (SGD)
method to update θ as
∇
θ
L(θ)=E [δ∇
θ
Q(s
n
, a
n
|θ)] , (87)
where
L(θ)=E
&

∗
(s
n
, a
n
|θ
′
) − Q(s
n
, a
n
|θ))
2
'
, (88)
is the loss function of the estimation Q-network. By updating
θ with θ
′
for each round training, we can avoid oscillation and
divergence.
On this basis, we use action a
n
with the maximum Q-value
in the estimation Q-network to update action a
n+1
with the
maximum Q-value in the target Q-network, i.e.,
Q
∗
(s
n+1
, a
n+1
|θ
′
)=Q
∗
(s
n+1
, argmaxQ(s
n+1
, a
n+1
|θ)|θ
′
),
(89)
which helps mitigate the overestimation of Q-values that can
arise from greedy exploitation in an expansive state space.
Additionally, we utilize a dueling network to evaluate the
state, as certain computational decisions may be equivalent,
rendering the state either favorable or unfavorable regardless of
the action chosen within the expansive state space. Speciﬁcally,
we use the average Q-value
¯
Q(s
n
, a
n
|θ) to estimate states,
which as follows
¯
Q(s
n
, a
n
|θ)=V (s
n
|θ)
+
⎛
⎝

n
, a
n
|θ) −
1
|A|

a
n+1
∈A
A(s

n+1

⎞
⎠
, (90)
where A is the dimension of the action space. V (s
n
|θ) and
A(s
n
, a
n
|θ) represent the state value function and action ad-
vantage function, respectively.
b) TD3: The TD3 algorithm employs two identical networks
to estimate Q values, utilizing the smaller Q value as the update
target to address the issue of overestimation. Speciﬁcally, the
architecture of the TD3 network comprises two components:
the actor network and the critic network. ϑ and ϑ
−
denote
the network parameters of the current actor network and the
target actor network, respectively. τ
1
and τ
−
1
denote the network
parameters of the ﬁrst current critic network and the ﬁrst tar-
get critic network, respectively. τ
2
and τ
−
2
denote the network
parameters of the second current critic network and the second
target critic network, respectively.
In addition, the TD3 algorithm solves the problem of overes-
timation bias through the following three techniques to improve
performance. The ﬁrst technique adds noise to the target action
and moderately smooths the target strategy to alleviate overﬁt-
ting issues. The second technique is to reduce double Q learning.
In the TD3 network, both the critic target network and the current
network contain two twin networks, which can simultaneously
learn two Q-value functions and select the smaller Q-value as
the target Q-value for the Bellman error function. The third
technique is delayed policy update, which sets the critic network
to update every two times and the actor network to update again,
making the critic network more stable and reducing errors before
updating the actor network.
Regarding the update method for the actor network, speciﬁ-
cally, the TD3 network uses the Q-values generated by the ﬁrst
critic network to update the actor network.
For the critic network, a smaller Q value is obtained by com-
paring two current critic networks, combined with the reward to
generate the target value y, which is expressed as
y = r
n+1
+ γ min
i=1,2
Q
i

s
n+1
, a; τ
−
i

|
a=π(s
n+1
,ϑ
−
)
. (91)
For the experience replay buffer, randomly select X tuples
{s
m
, a
m
, r
m
, s
m+1
}to train the Critic network. The parameter
weight τ
i
of the Critic network is updated by minimizing the
loss function L(τ
i
), where the loss function is expressed as
L (τ
i
)=
1
X

m
[y − Q
i
(s
m
, a
m
; τ
i
)]
2
. (92)
The algorithm workﬂow framework based on D3QN-TD3 is
in detail summarized in Algorithm
1. At the beginning of each
training iteration, the agent observes and collects environmental
channel information to obtain the initial system state s
0
.At
time step n, the state is input into the D3QN network to obtain
the discrete transmit/receiving MA selection A
n
and B
n
.The
TD3 network is used to obtain the continuous secondary BS
beamforming W
n
and RIS active phase shift Θ
n
. By executing
{A
n
, B
n
, W
n
, Θ
n
}and interact with the environment to obtain
reward r
n
and the next state s
n+1
, and continuously train until
the algorithm converges.
3) Computational Complexity Analysis: To evaluate the efﬁ-
ciency of the proposed solution, we mainly analyze the compu-
tational complexity of the Algorithm
1. Firstly, we analyze the
computational complexity of the optimization of transmit beam-
forming and RIS phase shift. It is obvious that the update for W
and Θ dominate the computational cost, whose complexities are
of order O{M
4.5
}and O{C
1
C
2
(M
4
√
L + M
3
)}, respectively,
where C
1
and C
2
is the number of the loops of outer-layer and
inner-layer in PDD framework, respectively
[85], [86]. Thus,
the overall complexity of the AO-based algorithm is of order
O{M
4.5
+ C
1
C
2
(M
4
√
L + M
3
)}. Next, we analyze the com-
putational complexity of D3QN-TD3 algorithm, which adopts
ofﬂine training and online execution policy. In both ofﬂine
training and online execution, the mapping from states to actions
is realized by DNNs, whose computational complexity is given
as O
J
= O(

J
j=1
o
j
o
j+1
), where J and o
j
are the number of
layers and the number of neurons at the j-th layer, respectively.
In ofﬂine training, with T training times, N training steps, and
Z training data, the computational complexity is calculated as
O(

J
j=1
o
j
o
j+1
TNZ). As the training process is performed

Algorithm 1: AO-based D3QN-TD3 Algorithm for Solving
Problem.
1: Initialization: Experience memory B, θ and θ
′
of the
evaluation the Q-networks Q(·) and target network
Q
∗
(·), the training actor network parameter ϑ and the
target actor network parameter ϑ
−
, the ﬁrst training
critic network parameter τ
1
and the ﬁrst target critic1
network parameter τ
−
1
, the second training critic
network parameter τ
2
and the second target critic
network parameter τ
−
2
.
2: for each episode do
3: Initialize the system state s
0
;
4: repeat
5: Obtain J by solving
(44);
6: Obtain W by solving (54);
7: Obtain Θ by solving
(76);
8: until Problem
(43) in coverged
9: for n =0, 1,...,N do
10: Collect current W, Θ, A, and B to observe state
s
n
.
11: Obtain action a
n
= {a
1
n
, a
2
n
} from D3QN and
actor network in TD3, respectively;
12: Store {s
n
, a
n
, r
n
, s
n+1
} in experience memory
B;
13: s
n+1
= s
n
;
14: Select a random mini-batch of samples from B;
15: Perform SGD to update D3QN network parameter
θ;
16: Obtain the loss function for the training critic
network according to
(91);
17: Update the actor policy by the sampled policy
gradient;
18: Update training actor network parameter ϑ and
training critic network parameters τ
1
and τ
2
,
respectively;
19: Update D3QN target network parameter θ
′
;
20: Update target critic network parameter ϑ
−
and
target actor network parameters τ
−
1
and τ
−
2
,
respectively;
21: end for
22: end for
ofﬂine, we can realize real-time online execution for the com-
plex industrial tasks, wherein the computational complexity is
only O
J
.
V. S IMULATION RESULTS
In this section, the simulation results validate the performance
of MA-enabled secure transmission for active RIS-aided ISAC
systems. Communication users and the eavesdropping target are
equipped with a single FPA. The transmit and receive region
for the MA is modeled as a 2D square, and the minimum
distance interval between each pair of MA is deﬁned as D
1,min
=
D
2,min
= λ/2, the transmission area size is set to A, where
λ =0.105 m represents the carrier wavelength. The locations
of ISAC BS and active RIS are set to (0 m, 0 m, 3 m) and (0 m,
20 m, 3 m), respectively. The communication users are located in
a circle with the center at (10 m, 20 m, 0 m) and a radius of 5 m. A
geometric channel model is adopted, where the numbers of trans-
mit and receive paths are equal, i.e. L
t
= L
r
= L
k
t
= L
k
r
=6,
∀k
[87]. According to reference [65], the path response matrix
Σ
k
and Σ are a diagonal matrix, with each diagonal element
following a distribution CN (0,c
0
d
−α
/L
0
), where c
0
= −40
dB denotes the channel gain at 1m and α =2.8 is the pathloss
exponent, respectively. The elevation angle and azimuth angle
(AoA/AoD) of the user/target (RIS) are treated as independent
and identically distributed random variables, each following a
uniform distribution. To simplify the analysis, it is assumed that
the noise variance of all communication and radar devices, as
well as RIS, is equal, i.e., σ
2
k
= σ
2
r
= σ
2
z
= −90 dBm, where
1 ≤ k ≤ K. Unless otherwise speciﬁed, the number of users,
the number of transmit MA and receive MAs, the transmit
power budget of BS, the communication SINR requirement, the
number of active RIS elements, the power threshold of active
RIS, and the CRB threshold are set to K =4, M = N =6,
P
BS
= 30 dBm, Γ=5dB,L = 128, P
RIS
= 10 dBm, and δ =
0.01, respectively.
The experiments took place on a Windows platform equipped
with an Intel Core i7 processor, a GeForce 4070 graphics card,
and 16 GB of RAM. In the proposed D3QN-TD3 algorithm
framework, D3QN network contains three hidden layers, and
actor network and critic network in TD3 have two hidden layers.
The learning rate of D3QN is set as 0.01, the learning rates of
the actor network and the critic network are set as 0.001 and
0.002, respectively. The discount factor is set as γ =0.99, and
the experience replay buffer is L = 20000.
Furthermore, we consider ﬁve other benchmark approaches
for comparison with our proposed method, which are listed as
follows:
r
D3QN-TD3: Without considering AO algorithm for the
optimization of transmission beamforming and RIS phase
shifts, directly apply the method combining D3QN and
TD3 for the MA-assisted RIS-aided system.
r
FPA: The transmitting and receiving antennas are ﬁxed in
determined positions to achieve better SR performance.
r
Random position antenna (RPA): The transmitting and
receiving antennas are randomly distributed within the
available area.
r
Passive RIS: Passive RIS replaces active RIS to serve the
MA-enabled system and ensure SR performance.
r
Random phase shift (RPS): Each element on the active
RIS has an amplitude of unity but a random phase shift
following a uniform distribution within the range [0, 2π).
Fig.
3 illustrates the convergence behavior of the proposed
AO-based D3QN-TD3 method compared to D3QN-TD3 bench-
mark scheme. As the number of training rounds increases,
each curve gradually stabilizes, indicating that the algorithm
converges over time. Notably, the reward value of the AO-based
D3QN-TD3 method continues to increase and stabilizes after
90 iterations, whereas the D3QN-TD3 algorithm requires 150
iterations to achieve convergence. This demonstrates that the
AO-based D3QN-TD3 algorithm exhibits signiﬁcant advantages
Authorized licensed use limited to: Xi'an Univ of Posts & Telecom. Downloaded on March 17,2026 at 01:10:53 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.
6340 IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON NETWORK SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING, VOL. 13, 2026
Fig. 3. Convergence behavior of proposed AO-based D3QN-TD3 method.
Fig. 4. Sum SR versus the number of antennas M.
in both stability and training speed compared to the D3QN-TD3
algorithm. The accelerated initial training process of the pro-
posed algorithm contributes to this performance, as evidenced by
the increasing reward value with the addition of multiple MAs.
This correlation arises because the reward value is associated
with the SR, thus, as the system utilizes more MAs, the reward
value correspondingly increases.
Fig.
4 illustrates the sum SR behavior of versus the number
of antenna. As the number of training rounds increases, each
curve gradually stabilizes, indicating that the algorithm con-
verges over time. Notably, the reward value of the AO-based
D3QN-TD3 method continues to increase and stabilizes after
90 iterations, whereas the D3QN-TD3 algorithm requires 150
iterations to achieve convergence. This demonstrates that the
AO-based D3QN-TD3 algorithm exhibits signiﬁcant advantages
in both stability and training speed compared to the D3QN-TD3
algorithm. The accelerated initial training process of the pro-
posed algorithm contributes to the performance, as evidenced by
the increasing reward value with the addition of multiple MAs.
The correlation arises because the reward value is associated
Fig. 5. Sum SR versus the communication SINR requirement Γ.
Fig. 6. Sum SR versus the number of users K.
with the SR, thus, as the system utilizes more MAs, the reward
value correspondingly increases.
Fig.
5 depicts the sum SR behavior comparison for various
methods against the communication SINR requirement. The
results demonstrate that this scheme consistently outperforms
all benchmark methods under various communication SINR
requirement Γ. This indicates that when Γ is ﬁxed, the AO-based
D3QN-TD3 method achieves a higher lower bound on the
secrecy rate, thereby signiﬁcantly enhancing system security.
For example, Γ=10dB, the AO-based D3QN-TD3 method
achieves a secrecy rate for all users is 15.13bps/Hz. However, un-
der constrained transmission power budgets and radar estimation
constraints, the secure transmission power for communication
information imposes an upper limit, resulting in a slight decline
in the performance growth trend.
Fig.
6 demonstrates the sum SR behavior comparison for
various methods against the number of users K. As the number
of users increases, the Sum Secrecy Rate shows a signiﬁcant
upward trend. It is also demonstrated that the AO-based D3QN-
TD3 algorithm exhibits optimal security performance in the
Authorized licensed use limited to: Xi'an Univ of Posts & Telecom. Downloaded on March 17,2026 at 01:10:53 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.
BIAN et al.: MOVABLE ANTENNA-ENABLED SECURE TRANSMISSION FOR ACTIVE RIS-AIDED ISAC SYSTEMS 6341
Fig. 7. Sum SR versus the transmit power P
BS
.
MA-enabled Active RIS system. This is because the system
can effectively suppress information leakage to eavesdroppers
through beamforming and antenna movement, resulting in the
cumulative increase of the total secrecy rate with the growing
number of served users. Furthermore, this also reﬂects the
high-precision beamforming capability of the AO algorithm,
which minimizes information leakage and compensates for the
accuracy limitations of pure DRL in handling continuous high-
dimensional beam optimization.
Fig.
7 illustrates the sum SR behavior comparison for various
methods against the transmit power P
BS
.AsP
BS
increases, the
sum SR performance exhibits a rapid growth trend. Furthermore,
the ISAC system utilizing an active RIS solution demonstrates
performance improvements of approximately 110% and 150%
over “passive RIS” and “RPS” schemes, respectively. This
highlights the critical importance of deploying RIS elements in
ISAC systems to prevent malicious eavesdropping. Additionally,
the MA scheme effectively leverages spatial DoF, resulting in
approximately 30% performance gains over the FPA scheme
across all scenarios. This enhancement can be not only attributed
to the higher transmission power enhancing the anti-interference
ability of the signal in complex environments, but also the
capture of higher spatial DoF by MA array.
Fig.
8 illustrates the sum SR behavior comparison for various
methods against the communication SINR requirement. The
results show that when the number of reﬂectors increased from
64 to 128, the system’s overall SR improved by 21.87 bps/Hz.
This indicates that appropriately increasing the number of RIS
elements can substantially enhance the SR. Furthermore, un-
der identical conditions of the number of RIS elements, the
introduction of active RIS scheme consistently outperforms the
“Passive RIS”. This is attributable to the signiﬁcant capacity
gains achieved by the active RIS system in mitigating the effects
of multipath fading.
In Fig.
9, we analyze the relationship between sum SR
behavior and the normalized moving region area of MAs. It
can be observed that the total movement distance of MAs
increases linearly with the size of the movement area, and
the proposed scheme can signiﬁcantly enhance security per-
formance when compared to the “FPA” and “RPA” schemes.
Fig. 8. Sum SR versus the number of RIS elements L.
Fig. 9. Sum SR versus the normalized moving region size of antennas A/λ.
For example, when the normalized moving region area equals
4, the sum SR achieved by the “FPA” scheme is 14.21 bps/Hz
while that achieved by the proposed scheme is approximately
17.56 bps/Hz. The phenomenon occurs because a larger recep-
tion area provides greater ﬂexibility for optimizing MA posi-
tions, enabling them to relocate to areas with improved channel
conditions and thereby enhancing system security. However, it
is important to note that the performance improvement derived
from expanding the movable area gradually diminishes as the
normalized moving region area increases.
Finally, Fig.
10 describes the relationship between sum SR
behavior and the normalized moving region area of MAs.
Compared to the “PPA” solution, the MA approach capitalizes
on its spatial multiplexing gain advantage to achieve superior
performance in secure transmission, irrespective of radar func-
tionality. As the CRB threshold δ increases, the transmission of
safe rates tends to stabilize. The phenomenon occurs because
progressively relaxing constraints on radar performance allows
for a greater allocation of transmission power to radar estima-
tion, consequently reducing the power available for base station
information transmission.
Authorized licensed use limited to: Xi'an Univ of Posts & Telecom. Downloaded on March 17,2026 at 01:10:53 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.
6342 IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON NETWORK SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING, VOL. 13, 2026
Fig. 10. Sum SR versus the CRB threshold δ.
VI. CONCLUSION
In this paper, we studied a novel MA-enabled active RIS-aided
ISAC systems to enhance the secure transmission. The objective
of this study was to maximize the minimum SR by the joint
design of transmit beamforming and phase shift of RIS, as
well as the MA discrete location. Given the non-convexity of
the objective function and the difﬁculty in solving it using
traditional methods, we developed an AO-based D3QN-TD3
method to handle the hybrid action space issue. Speciﬁcally, the
effective integration of AO optimization with the D3QN-TD3
algorithm framework resolves issues of algorithmic process-
ing difﬁculties and getting stuck in local optima. Numerical
results indicated that the proposed MA-assisted ISAC system
signiﬁcantly outperforms conventional FPA scheme in terms
of secure transmission. In addition, it is worth noting that the
algorithm we proposed is highly effective in practical scenarios
involving discrete antenna positioning. In the future, we will
consider comparing the performance upper limits of discrete
MA optimization and continuous MA optimization for ISAC
systems. Additionally, we will focus on investigating the impact
of 6D MA-assisted ISAC systems on secure transmission.

Hongyi Bian is currently working toward the Ph.D.
degree with the School of Information and Com-
munication Engineering, Hainan University, Haikou,
China. His research interests include ISAC, physical
layer security, and intelligent reffecting surface.
Feilong Zhao is currently a Senior Engineer and Gen-
eral Manager of Hainan Mobile Innovation Center.
His research interests include electromagnetic wave
propagation and its applications, mobile communica-
tions, and computing power networks.
Minghao Chen (Member, IEEE) received the B.S.
degree in communication engineering and the Ph.D.
degree in electric information technology from North
China Electric Power University, Beijing, China. He
is currently an Associate Researcher with the School
of Information and Communication, Hainan Univer-
sity, Haikou, China, focusing on the research of wire-
less communications, reinforcement learning, and
UAV-enabled communication. He has been a reviewer
of IEEE T
RANSACTIONS ON GREEN COMMUNICA-
TIONS AND NETWORKING and IEEE TRANSACTIONS
ON INDUSTRIAL ELECTRONICS.
Lingling Liu (Member, IEEE) received the Ph.D.
degree in computer science and technology from Jilin
University, Changchun, China, in 2024. Since 2024,
she has been with the School of Information and Com-
munication Engineering, Hainan University, Haikou,
China. Her research interests include UAV networks,
space-air-ground Integrated netowrk, wireless sensor
networks, reconﬁgurable intelligence surface, and an-
tenna array optimization.
Yu Yao (Member, IEEE) received the M.S. and Ph.D.
degrees in information and communication engineer-
ing from Southeast University, Nanjing, China, in
2010 and 2015, respectively. He is currently a Pro-
fessor with the School of Information and Com-
munication Engineering, Hainan University, Haikou,
China. From 2019 to 2020, he was a Visiting Scholar
with the Department of Electrical Engineering and
Information Technology, University of Naples Fed-
erico Il, Naples, Italy. His research interests in-
clude wireless communications, satellite communi-
cations, integrated communication and sensing, Internet of Things, and secrecy
communications.
Hao Jiang (Senior Member, IEEE) received the B.S.
and M.S. degrees in electrical and information engi-
neering from the Nanjing University of Information
Science and Technology, Nanjing, China, in 2012
and 2015, respectively, and the Ph.D. degree from
Southeast University, Nanjing, in 2019. From 2017 to
2018, he was a Visiting Student with the Department
of Electrical Engineering, Columbia University, New
York, NY, USA. Since 2019, he has been a Professor
with the School of Information Science and Engineer-
ing, Nanjing University of Information Science and
Technology. His research interests include wireless channel measurements and
modeling, B5G wireless communication networks, signal processing, machine
learning, and AI-driven technologies.
Feng Shu (Senior Member, IEEE) received the
B.S. degree from Fuyang Teaching College, Fuyang,
China, in 1994, the M.S. degree from Xidian Uni-
versity, Xi’an, China, in 1997, and the Ph.D. degree
from Southeast University, Nanjing, China, in 2002.
He was a Visiting Postdoctoral Fellow with the Uni-
versity of Texas at Dallas, Richardson, TX, USA,
and a Visiting Scholar with the Royal Melbourne
Institute of Technology, Melbourne VIC, Australia.
From 2005 to 2020, he was with the Nanjin University
of Science and Technology, being promoted to Full
Professor in 2013. Since 2020, he has been with the School of Information
and Communication Engineering, Hainan University, where he is currently a
Professor and a Supervisor of Ph.D. and graduate students. He has authored
or coauthored more than 400 archival journal papers, including more than
150 on IEEE journals and 320 SCI - indexed papers with more than 11 000
citations. He holds one US patent and about 50 Chinese patents. His research
interests include machine learning, wireless networks, wireless location, and
array signal processing. He was awarded the Hainan First Prize for Natural
Science in 2025, First Prize for Science and Technology of the China Institute
of Communications in 2025, Fujian Third Prize for Natural Science in 2024,
Leading-Talent Plan of Hainan Province in 2020, Fujian Hundred-Talent Plan
of Fujian Province in 2018, and Minjiang Scholar Chair Professor in 2015. From
2019 to 2024, he was ranked in the 2% Top Scientists by Stanford/Elsevier, and
enters the list of 1% Top scientists in 2024. He is the Editor of IEEE W
IRELESS
COMMUNICATIONS LETTERS, IEEE SYSTEMS JOURNAL, and IEEE ACCESS.He
is the Guest Editor of CJA, Journal of Electronics and Information Technology,
and IET Communications.
Authorized licensed use limited to: Xi'an Univ of Posts & Telecom. Downloaded on March 17,2026 at 01:10:53 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.
